Council, this case asks a narrow, verifiable question under a preponderance standard: Is it more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through its own official channels, with NFTs, promotional items, point systems, and partner/community tokens expressly excluded?

Plaintiff’s theory
- Polymarket did launch an official fungible token before the cutoff and said so through its own channels. The decisive proof will pair (1) authoritative, time‑stamped communications attributable to Polymarket with (2) on‑chain evidence of a fungible token whose issuance and initial control trace to Polymarket, and (3) indications of public availability or distribution consistent with a launch.

What “official fungible token” and “launch” mean here
- Fungible token: a transferable cryptoasset on a public blockchain (e.g., an ERC‑20/SPL‑like token), not an NFT and not a non‑transferable points balance.
- Official: created, issued, or adopted by Polymarket (or its operating entity) and publicly communicated by Polymarket’s own official channels (e.g., its website, blog, docs, product UI, verified social accounts, or press releases under its control). Third‑party announcements alone are not sufficient.
- Launch: a public release to users or markets—e.g., announcement of the token with a contract address or ticker; enabling transferability, distribution, listing, redemption, or similar public availability. Mere internal testing or silent contract deployment would not, by itself, be enough; conversely, a clear official announcement plus observable availability would satisfy it.

Key factual questions that will decide the case
1) Did Polymarket’s official channels, before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027, communicate that a fungible token of its own was launched, including its name/ticker or contract address?
2) Is there an on‑chain fungible token whose deployer/owner or mint authority is credibly attributable to Polymarket (e.g., via a known corporate wallet, multisig, or a contract verified and linked by Polymarket)?
3) Was the token actually made publicly available (listing, distribution, redemption, or transferability) before the cutoff?
4) Do any materials indicate the asset was instead an NFT, an off‑chain/non‑transferable point/loyalty system, a purely promotional voucher, or a token launched by a partner/community rather than by Polymarket itself?
5) Timing: Do the best‑evidence timestamps (official posts, archived pages, block/transaction times) place both the announcement and the availability before the cutoff when converted to PST?

Proof that will satisfy the burden
- Primary: A Polymarket‑controlled announcement (site/blog/docs/app UI notice/verified social) before the cutoff explicitly presenting a fungible token as a Polymarket token and identifying its contract or ticker, paired with chain‑explorer data showing the token existed and was usable or distributed before the cutoff.
- Corroboration: Archived snapshots of those pages; consistent messaging across multiple official channels; press coverage quoting or linking to Polymarket’s announcement; code or repositories under Polymarket control referencing the token address; attestations or signatures that bind the deployer to Polymarket.
- Authentication: Evidence tying the communicating accounts and deployer wallets to Polymarket (domain control, verified handles, on‑site linking, or cryptographic signatures).

Proof that would defeat the claim
- Only points, NFTs, or promotional credits existed before the cutoff; any fungible token materialized only after the cutoff; or the token in question was launched by a third party with no official adoption by Polymarket’s channels. Also defeating would be clear official statements before the cutoff that no token had been launched.

Why this resolves the real dispute
- The dispute turns on whether “official” and “launch” are met by Polymarket’s own communications and control over a fungible token, not by speculation or third‑party hype. The evidence outlined above decisively answers attribution, asset type, and timing against the defined exclusions.

Next steps to build the record
- Collect and authenticate: (a) Polymarket’s website/blog/docs/app UI notices; (b) posts from verified official social accounts; (c) archived snapshots prior to the cutoff; (d) chain‑explorer records for the token (contract creation, minting, ownership, distributions) and the deployer’s provenance; (e) any official press releases or emails issued by Polymarket; and (f) corroborating reputable press that cites Polymarket’s own communications. Convert all timestamps to PST and correlate announcement and availability. Where necessary, obtain cryptographic proofs (e.g., signed messages) that a deployer or multisig is controlled by Polymarket.

Under a preponderance standard, a coherent set of official communications plus matching on‑chain facts will be sufficient; the absence of such official communication, or the presence only of excluded categories, will not.

New version available: v0.72.1 (installed v0.55.1). Run: `npm i -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`

Council, the proposition places a clear, binary claim under a preponderance standard: that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, with NFTs, promotional items, tokenized points, and partner/community tokens expressly excluded.

Defense theory
- The most likely state of the world is that no such officially launched, Polymarket‑branded fungible token—communicated by Polymarket itself and made publicly available as a launch—occurred before the cutoff. Alternatively, any items that might be cited (points, per‑market instruments, third‑party tokens, or speculative posts by others) either fall within the exclusions or fail the “official + launch + public communication by Polymarket” elements.
- Because the claim is conjunctive, plaintiff must prove: (1) the asset is a fungible token, (2) it is official to Polymarket, and (3) it was launched and publicly communicated by Polymarket before the PST cutoff. Ambiguity or gaps on any single element defeat the proposition under a preponderance standard.

Where the real dispute lies
- "Official": whether control, authorship, or adoption can be reliably attributed to Polymarket (or its operating entity) rather than to unrelated deployers, community members, or partners. Mere association or platform usage of external tokens is not “official.”
- "Launch": whether there was a public release, not quiet deployment or internal testing. Launch requires clear availability (e.g., distribution/transferability/listing) combined with explicit communication by Polymarket.
- "Publicly communicated through official channels": whether Polymarket’s own website, blog, documentation, product UI, or verified social accounts conveyed the token’s launch, not just third‑party articles or rumors. Screenshots without provenance, reposts, or paraphrases are insufficient.
- Timing: whether both the communication and availability occurred before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. All timestamps must be converted to PST, and archived materials should be checked against that standard.

Factual questions that will decide the case
1) Did Polymarket itself, on a Polymarket‑controlled channel, state that a Polymarket fungible token had launched before the cutoff, identifying the token (ticker or contract) and its intended role? If the communication is hedged, preview, or non‑launch language, it fails.
2) Is there on‑chain evidence of a fungible token whose deployer/owner, mint authority, or verified contract is credibly attributable to Polymarket (e.g., a corporate wallet, multisig, or domain‑verified contract) before the cutoff? Mere presence of a token with “Polymarket” in its name is not enough.
3) Was the token actually made publicly available prior to the cutoff (e.g., transfers enabled, distribution, redemption, or listing) as opposed to an inert contract, canary deployment, or internal test?
4) Do cited items fall within explicit exclusions—NFTs, promotional credits, non‑transferable or off‑chain point systems, or tokens launched by partners/communities rather than by Polymarket itself?
5) Are there official statements by Polymarket before the cutoff that indicate no token launch occurred or that clarify points/credits are not fungible tokens? Such statements, if present, would be highly probative.

Proof that would satisfy plaintiff’s burden (and what the council should expect to see)
- A Polymarket‑controlled announcement prior to the cutoff explicitly describing a Polymarket fungible token as launched, with a contract address or ticker; plus chain‑explorer data confirming creation, control by Polymarket, and public availability before the cutoff; corroborated by archived snapshots and consistent messaging across official channels.
- Strong attribution linking the deployer or mint authority to Polymarket (domain‑verified source, cross‑linked addresses from official properties, or cryptographic attestations). Merely inferring attribution from naming or hearsay is inadequate.

Proof that defeats the proposition
- Absence of any pre‑cutoff official Polymarket communication announcing a fungible token launch; or communications that reference only excluded categories (NFTs, promos, points) or non‑launch previews.
- On‑chain tokens bearing the Polymarket name but deployed by unaffiliated third parties, or by partners in collaborations, without Polymarket’s official adoption and announcement.
- Contract deployments before the cutoff with no public communication by Polymarket, or with no public availability until after the cutoff.
- Official statements by Polymarket clarifying that no token had launched by the cutoff or that any referenced asset is not a fungible token.

Forward‑looking plan to build the record
- Collect authoritative Polymarket‑controlled materials: website/blog/docs, app UI notices, verified social posts, and any official press pages; preserve with timestamps and archive hashes; convert times to PST.
- Obtain chain‑explorer records for any purported token: contract creation time, ownership, minting, transfers, and verifications; map controlling wallets and look for links from official Polymarket properties.
- Compile a synchronized timeline correlating official communications with on‑chain facts; note any gaps between announcement and actual public availability.
- Identify and exclude artifacts that are points, NFTs, promotional rewards, or partner/community tokens; document why each exclusion applies.
- Seek contradictory or clarifying official statements by Polymarket pre‑cutoff regarding the existence or non‑existence of a token.

Why plaintiff is unlikely to carry the burden
- The proposition requires a precise convergence of three elements—official authorship/adoption, an actual launch, and explicit pre‑cutoff communication by Polymarket. In practice, purported evidence often falters on attribution (third‑party deployments), category (points or promos), or timing (either post‑cutoff release or pre‑cutoff silence). Under a preponderance standard, unresolved ambiguity on any element means the proposition should be rejected.

Requested council focus
- Demand primary, Polymarket‑controlled communications and verifiable on‑chain attribution before the cutoff; treat third‑party chatter, screenshots without provenance, or ambiguous language as insufficient. If the best evidence is equivocal or incomplete, the proposition fails.

- Because the claim requires official authorship/adoption, an actual launch, and explicit pre‑cutoff communication by Polymarket, the chances of ambiguity on attribution, asset category, or timing are high. - On‑chain tokens bearing “Polymarket” but deployed by unaffiliated third parties/partners without Polymarket’s official adoption and announcement. 
- Pre‑cutoff deployments without Polymarket communication, or with no public availability until after the cutoff. 
- Official Polymarket statements pre‑cutoff that no token had launched or that any referenced asset is not a fungible token.

Record‑building steps
- Collect Polymarket‑controlled materials (website/blog/docs, app UI notices, verified social, official press); preserve timestamps and archives; convert to PST. 
- Obtain chain‑explorer records (creation time, ownership, minting, transfers, verifications); map controlling wallets; look for links from official Polymarket properties. 
- Build a timeline aligning communications with on‑chain facts; note gaps between announcement and availability. 
- Identify and exclude points, NFTs, promos, or partner/community tokens, documenting why each exclusion applies. 
- Search for pre‑cutoff Polymarket statements clarifying absence of a token or non‑fungibility of points.

Why plaintiff is unlikely to prevail
Because the claim requires official authorship/adoption, an actual launch, and explicit pre‑cutoff communication by Polymarket, ambiguity on attribution, asset category, or timing is likely. Under a preponderance standard, unresolved ambiguity on any element defeats the proposition.

payload.text:
Council,

Issue and burden
Under a preponderance standard, the proposition is satisfied if it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched (ii) an official (iii) fungible token and (iv) publicly communicated that launch via its own channels, with NFTs, promos, point systems, and partner/community tokens excluded.

What the record shows (official communications and chain‑attribution)
1) Polymarket’s own documentation identifies “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” describing it as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable and backed by USDC. That is a fungible token, not an NFT or non‑transferable points. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
2) Polymarket’s Contracts page—described there as the “single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform”—lists pUSD under Collateral Contracts and gives its Polygon address. This is a Polymarket‑controlled channel tying a specific fungible token contract to Polymarket. (PX‑2)
3) Polymarket’s public changelog and V2 migration guide state that a “new collateral token (pUSD)” shipped with the CLOB V2 upgrade and that the production go‑live occurred on April 28, 2026 (announced in an April 17, 2026 entry; confirmed in the April 28, 2026 entry). Those are Polymarket‑authored pages communicating the token’s rollout to the public. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
4) A Polygonscan page titled “Polymarket: pUSD Token” at the same address corroborates on‑chain existence and usage; while secondary, it aligns with Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” listing. (PX‑1)

Timing normalized to PST
Polymarket’s April 28, 2026 go‑live time is stated around 11:00 UTC. That equals 04:00 Pacific Daylight Time on April 28, 2026, well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. (See Time Normalization technical note.)

Elements applied to the proposition
- Fungible token: The record calls pUSD a “standard ERC‑20” with transferability and decimals. ERC‑20 tokens are fungible by construction. (PX‑1)
- Official: The token is named “Polymarket USD,” appears on Polymarket’s “single source of truth” Contracts page with its address, and is described across multiple Polymarket‑controlled docs pages. That is official authorship and adoption. (PX‑2)
- Launch: Polymarket publicly announced the token as part of a platform upgrade and stated the production go‑live date. Post‑go‑live, the token was in use as platform collateral. That is a public release with availability, not a silent or test deployment. (PX‑1)
- Public communication on official channels: All cited communications are on docs.polymarket.com, a Polymarket‑controlled property (changelog, migration guide, concepts and contracts pages). (PX‑1, PX‑2)

Addressing the exclusions and likely defenses
- Not an NFT: The token is explicitly ERC‑20 and platform‑collateral; NFTs are excluded by definition. (PX‑1)
- Not a promotional credit or point system: The docs characterize pUSD as a transferable ERC‑20 used for all trading collateral, not as non‑transferable points or limited‑use promo credits. (PX‑1)
- Not a partner/community token: The token is branded “Polymarket USD,” documented and addressed on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” page. No collaboration framing is presented. (PX‑2)
- “Collateral token ≠ official token” objection: The proposition turns on official authorship/adoption and public communication of a fungible token launch—not on whether the token is used for collateral versus speculation. Polymarket expressly “shipped … a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” and put it into production. That is an official fungible token launch by Polymarket, satisfying all elements. (PX‑1)

What our investigation found versus what we infer
- Found (now preserved in PX‑1/PX‑2): Polymarket’s own pages naming, describing, and addressing pUSD; public changelog/migration entries giving the April 28, 2026 production launch; and contracts attribution tying pUSD to Polymarket.
- Infer: Given those communications and the contracts listing, Polymarket both launched and publicly communicated an official fungible token before the cutoff; the intended meaning of “official” in the proposition is met by Polymarket’s control of the docs and contracts registry, the Polymarket branding of the token, and the production go‑live.

Conclusion
On this record, it is more likely than not that Polymarket launched an official fungible token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels on April 28, 2026, well before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. All elements are met; the exclusions do not apply. The Council should find the proposition true under a preponderance standard.

Exhibits offered
PX‑1: primary-evidence.txt (official page quotes and go‑live entries)
PX‑2: official-source-record.txt (source index with quotations and contract‑address listing)

Technical report offered
TR‑1: Time Normalization (UTC→Pacific) for cited events: April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT; Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST is seven hours behind UTC.

payload.text:
Council,

Issue and burden
The proposition is conjunctive. Plaintiff must show, by a preponderance of the evidence, that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (1) launched (2) an official (3) fungible token and (4) publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, with the listed exclusions.

Defense position
On this record, plaintiff has not crossed 50% on two elements: “launched” and “officially released by Polymarket.” The materials credibly show Polymarket documented a new collateral instrument called “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” and rolled it out with CLOB V2. They do not reliably establish that Polymarket launched a Polymarket token in the sense required by the proposition—i.e., that Polymarket itself issued/released a fungible cryptoasset as a token launch, as opposed to introducing an internal collateral mechanism within a backend upgrade.

What the record shows (and what it does not)
1) Polymarket docs describe pUSD as the collateral token used for all trading, a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, backed by USDC. (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)
2) A Polymarket “Contracts” page, styled as a “single source of truth,” lists a pUSD contract address. (official‑source‑record.txt)
3) Changelog and a V2 migration page say CLOB V2 went live April 28, 2026 and reference “a new collateral token (pUSD).” (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)
4) A Polygonscan page is cited as corroboration. (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)

What is missing for plaintiff’s burden
A) Launch vs. infrastructure change: Nothing in the cited Polymarket pages frames this as a “token launch.” The communications characterize pUSD as a collateral component of an infrastructure release, not a stand‑alone token product launch. There is no Polymarket announcement that “we launched a Polymarket token,” no distribution/issuance details, and no evidence of a public release separate from enabling the exchange backend. (primary‑evidence.txt)
B) “Officially released by Polymarket”: The record lacks chain‑level attribution that Polymarket deployed or controls the pUSD token contract (deployer wallet, admin/mint authority, or cryptographic attestation). A link from an official docs page to an address is adoption/usage evidence; it is not proof that Polymarket “released” the token as the issuer. (official‑source‑record.txt)
C) Timing authentication: Plaintiff relies on Polymarket‑authored dates in a changelog and migration page. The preserved copies are post‑run captures without immutable archive timestamps or server headers tying those statements to April 2026. No block‑timestamp evidence for contract creation or first public availability is in the record. (official‑source‑record.txt; unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt)

Why these gaps matter to the elements
- “Launched”: The proposition requires a launch of a token, not merely the introduction of an internal collateral unit as part of a code upgrade. A reasonable reading of “launch” entails a public release of a token as a product/asset—identified issuance, availability, and circulation—not just a backend refactor. The record never uses “launch a token” language and supplies no issuance/distribution facts. At best, it shows that Polymarket enabled trading collateral labeled pUSD when V2 went live. That is consistent with an infrastructure change, not a token launch.
- “Officially released by Polymarket”: Even if pUSD exists and is used, the record does not demonstrate Polymarket authored/deployed the ERC‑20, held the mint/admin keys, or otherwise “released” it as its issuer. Without that, plaintiff has not proved this was a Polymarket token release rather than a Polymarket‑documented integration or wrapper.
- “Publicly communicated through official channels”: The docs are indeed official channels, but they communicate an exchange upgrade that includes a collateral token—again pointing to infrastructure, not an official token launch.

Addressing plaintiff’s points
- Fungibility: We do not contest that an ERC‑20 is fungible. The dispute is “official” and “launch.”
- Single‑source‑of‑truth page: This is strong evidence of Polymarket adopting/using the instrument; it is not sufficient to prove Polymarket released/issued a token.
- Explorer corroboration: The record describes but does not preserve immutable on‑chain creation timestamps, deployer attribution, or governance of the contract. Without those, chain corroboration does not cure the authorship/launch gap.
- Timing: Even crediting April 28, 2026 as V2 go‑live, plaintiff still must prove a token “launch” by Polymarket. The record does not do so.

Inference and standard
Where language is ambiguous, the burden stays with plaintiff. On this packet, a fair tie‑breaker is to require clear evidence that Polymarket actually launched a Polymarket token (issuance/release) and said so. Because the current proof is equally consistent with an internal collateral mechanism shipped in a backend upgrade, the probability that the proposition is true does not exceed 50%.

Conclusion
Plaintiff proved that Polymarket documented and adopted a collateral ERC‑20 called pUSD in connection with CLOB V2. Plaintiff did not prove, by a preponderance, that Polymarket launched an official fungible token within the meaning of the proposition—i.e., that Polymarket itself released/issued a token and publicly communicated that launch. The Council should find the proposition not proven on this record.

Record references
- primary‑evidence.txt (PX‑1)
- official‑source‑record.txt (PX‑2)
- unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt (DX‑1)

New version available: v0.72.1 (installed v0.55.1). Run: `npm i -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`

Council,

The defense’s case turns on importing requirements that are not in the proposition. The proposition requires: before Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched an (ii) official (iii) fungible token and (iv) publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket channels, while excluding NFTs, promos, points, and partner/community tokens. It does not require (a) an ICO/sale, (b) chain‑level proof that Polymarket’s wallet deployed the contract, or (c) magic words like “token launch.”

1) “Launch” is satisfied by Polymarket’s own go‑live communication and availability

By a preponderance of the evidence, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—and publicly communicated that launch through its own channels. The Council should find the proposition true.

arguments.text:
Council,

Issue and burden
Under a preponderance standard, the proposition is satisfied if it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched (ii) an official (iii) fungible token and (iv) publicly communicated that launch via its own channels, with NFTs, promos, point systems, and partner/community tokens excluded.

What the record shows (official communications and chain‑attribution)
1) Polymarket’s own documentation identifies “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” describing it as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable and backed by USDC. That is a fungible token, not an NFT or non‑transferable points. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
2) Polymarket’s Contracts page—described there as the “single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform”—lists pUSD under Collateral Contracts and gives its Polygon address. This is a Polymarket‑controlled channel tying a specific fungible token contract to Polymarket. (PX‑2)
3) Polymarket’s public changelog and V2 migration guide state that a “new collateral token (pUSD)” shipped with the CLOB V2 upgrade and that the production go‑live occurred on April 28, 2026 (announced in an April 17, 2026 entry; confirmed in the April 28, 2026 entry). Those are Polymarket‑authored pages communicating the token’s rollout to the public. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
4) A Polygonscan page titled “Polymarket: pUSD Token” at the same address corroborates on‑chain existence and usage; while secondary, it aligns with Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” listing. (PX‑1)

Timing normalized to PST
Polymarket’s April 28, 2026 go‑live time is stated around 11:00 UTC. That equals 04:00 Pacific Daylight Time on April 28, 2026, well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. (See Time Normalization technical note.)

Elements applied to the proposition
- Fungible token: The record calls pUSD a “standard ERC‑20” with transferability and decimals. ERC‑20 tokens are fungible by construction. (PX‑1)
- Official: The token is named “Polymarket USD,” appears on Polymarket’s “single source of truth” Contracts page with its address, and is described across multiple Polymarket‑controlled docs pages. That is official authorship and adoption. (PX‑2)
- Launch: Polymarket publicly announced the token as part of a platform upgrade and stated the production go‑live date. Post‑go‑live, the token was in use as platform collateral. That is a public release with availability, not a silent or test deployment. (PX‑1)
- Public communication on official channels: All cited communications are on docs.polymarket.com, a Polymarket‑controlled property (changelog, migration guide, concepts and contracts pages). (PX‑1, PX‑2)

Addressing the exclusions and likely defenses
- Not an NFT: The token is explicitly ERC‑20 and platform‑collateral; NFTs are excluded by definition. (PX‑1)
- Not a promotional credit or point system: The docs characterize pUSD as a transferable ERC‑20 used for all trading collateral, not as non‑transferable points or limited‑use promo credits. (PX‑1)
- Not a partner/community token: The token is branded “Polymarket USD,” documented and addressed on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” page. No collaboration framing is presented. (PX‑2)
- “Collateral token ≠ official token” objection: The proposition turns on official authorship/adoption and public communication of a fungible token launch—not on whether the token is used for collateral versus speculation. Polymarket expressly “shipped … a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” and put it into production. That is an official fungible token launch by Polymarket, satisfying all elements. (PX‑1)

What our investigation found versus what we infer
- Found (now preserved in PX‑1/PX‑2): Polymarket’s own pages naming, describing, and addressing pUSD; public changelog/migration entries giving the April 28, 2026 production launch; and contracts attribution tying pUSD to Polymarket.
- Infer: Given those communications and the contracts listing, Polymarket both launched and publicly communicated an official fungible token before the cutoff; the intended meaning of “official” in the proposition is met by Polymarket’s control of the docs and contracts registry, the Polymarket branding of the token, and the production go‑live.

Conclusion
On this record, it is more likely than not that Polymarket launched an official fungible token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels on April 28, 2026, well before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. All elements are met; the exclusions do not apply. The Council should find the proposition true under a preponderance standard.

Exhibits offered
PX‑1: primary-evidence.txt (official page quotes and go‑live entries)
PX‑2: official-source-record.txt (source index with quotations and contract‑address listing)

Technical report offered
TR‑1: Time Normalization (UTC→Pacific) for cited events: April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT; Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST is seven hours behind UTC.

Council,

Issue and burden
The proposition is conjunctive. Plaintiff must show, by a preponderance of the evidence, that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (1) launched (2) an official (3) fungible token and (4) publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, with the listed exclusions.

Defense position
On this record, plaintiff has not crossed 50% on two elements: “launched” and “officially released by Polymarket.” The materials credibly show Polymarket documented a new collateral instrument called “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” and rolled it out with CLOB V2. They do not reliably establish that Polymarket launched a Polymarket token in the sense required by the proposition—i.e., that Polymarket itself issued/released a fungible cryptoasset as a token launch, as opposed to introducing an internal collateral mechanism within a backend upgrade.

What the record shows (and what it does not)
1) Polymarket docs describe pUSD as the collateral token used for all trading, a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, backed by USDC. (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)
2) A Polymarket “Contracts” page, styled as a “single source of truth,” lists a pUSD contract address. (official‑source‑record.txt)
3) Changelog and a V2 migration page say CLOB V2 went live April 28, 2026 and reference “a new collateral token (pUSD).” (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)
4) A Polygonscan page is cited as corroboration. (primary‑evidence.txt; official‑source‑record.txt)

What is missing for plaintiff’s burden
A) Launch vs. infrastructure change: Nothing in the cited Polymarket pages frames this as a “token launch.” The communications characterize pUSD as a collateral component of an infrastructure release, not a stand‑alone token product launch. There is no Polymarket announcement that “we launched a Polymarket token,” no distribution/issuance details, and no evidence of a public release separate from enabling the exchange backend. (primary‑evidence.txt)
B) “Officially released by Polymarket”: The record lacks chain‑level attribution that Polymarket deployed or controls the pUSD token contract (deployer wallet, admin/mint authority, or cryptographic attestation). A link from an official docs page to an address is adoption/usage evidence; it is not proof that Polymarket “released” the token as the issuer. (official‑source‑record.txt)
C) Timing authentication: Plaintiff relies on Polymarket‑authored dates in a changelog and migration page. The preserved copies are post‑run captures without immutable archive timestamps or server headers tying those statements to April 2026. No block‑timestamp evidence for contract creation or first public availability is in the record. (official‑source‑record.txt; unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt)

Why these gaps matter to the elements
- “Launched”: The proposition requires a launch of a token, not merely the introduction of an internal collateral unit as part of a code upgrade. A reasonable reading of “launch” entails a public release of a token as a product/asset—identified issuance, availability, and circulation—not just a backend refactor. The record never uses “launch a token” language and supplies no issuance/distribution facts. At best, it shows that Polymarket enabled trading collateral labeled pUSD when V2 went live. That is consistent with an infrastructure change, not a token launch.
- “Officially released by Polymarket”: Even if pUSD exists and is used, the record does not demonstrate Polymarket authored/deployed the ERC‑20, held the mint/admin keys, or otherwise “released” it as its issuer. Without that, plaintiff has not proved this was a Polymarket token release rather than a Polymarket‑documented integration or wrapper.
- “Publicly communicated through official channels”: The docs are indeed official channels, but they communicate an exchange upgrade that includes a collateral token—again pointing to infrastructure, not an official token launch.

Addressing plaintiff’s points
- Fungibility: We do not contest that an ERC‑20 is fungible. The dispute is “official” and “launch.”
- Single‑source‑of‑truth page: This is strong evidence of Polymarket adopting/using the instrument; it is not sufficient to prove Polymarket released/issued a token.
- Explorer corroboration: The record describes but does not preserve immutable on‑chain creation timestamps, deployer attribution, or governance of the contract. Without those, chain corroboration does not cure the authorship/launch gap.
- Timing: Even crediting April 28, 2026 as V2 go‑live, plaintiff still must prove a token “launch” by Polymarket. The record does not do so.

Inference and standard
Where language is ambiguous, the burden stays with plaintiff. On this packet, a fair tie‑breaker is to require clear evidence that Polymarket actually launched a Polymarket token (issuance/release) and said so. Because the current proof is equally consistent with an internal collateral mechanism shipped in a backend upgrade, the probability that the proposition is true does not exceed 50%.

Conclusion
Plaintiff proved that Polymarket documented and adopted a collateral ERC‑20 called pUSD in connection with CLOB V2. Plaintiff did not prove, by a preponderance, that Polymarket launched an official fungible token within the meaning of the proposition—i.e., that Polymarket itself released/issued a token and publicly communicated that launch. The Council should find the proposition not proven on this record.

Record references

- official‑source‑record.txt (PX‑2)
- unresolved‑record‑gaps.txt (DX‑1)

closings.text:
Council,

Standard and question
The proposition succeeds if it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels, with NFTs, promos, point systems, and partner/community tokens excluded.

What the record establishes
1) Asset type and purpose: Polymarket’s own pUSD concept page describes “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as the collateral token used for all trading, a standard, transferable ERC‑20 on Polygon with decimals and USDC backing. (PX‑1)
2) Official attribution: Polymarket’s Contracts page—self‑described as the “single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform”—lists pUSD under Collateral Contracts with its Polygon address. (PX‑2)
3) Launch and timing: Polymarket’s changelog and V2 migration guide state that a “new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” shipped with the CLOB V2 upgrade, with production go‑live on April 28, 2026, and direct readers to the migration guide for pUSD. (PX‑1, PX‑2) TR‑1 converts ~11:00 UTC on April 28, 2026 to 04:00 PDT on April 28, 2026—well before the January 1, 2027 cutoff.
4) Corroboration: An explorer page labels the same address “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” aligning on‑chain existence with Polymarket’s own address listing. (PX‑1)

Application to each element
• Fungible token: ERC‑20 tokens are fungible by construction; the pUSD page also states transferability and decimals. (PX‑1)
• Official: The token is branded “Polymarket USD,” appears on Polymarket’s “single source of truth” Contracts page with its address, and is documented across multiple Polymarket‑controlled pages. (PX‑2)
• Launched: Polymarket’s own words—“shipped a new collateral token (pUSD)” and “CLOB V2 is live on production”—coupled with immediate platform use as mandatory collateral, are the ordinary‑language hallmarks of a launch: a public release with availability. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
• Publicly communicated through official channels: All communications are on docs.polymarket.com: the changelog, migration guide, concepts page, and contracts registry. (PX‑1, PX‑2)
• Exclusions: The record expressly characterizes pUSD as a standard, transferable ERC‑20 used for platform collateral—not an NFT, not a promotional credit, not a tokenized points system, and not a partner/community token. (PX‑1)

Addressing the defense’s strongest point
The defense says plaintiff failed to prove “officially released by Polymarket” because there is no deployer‑wallet or mint‑authority proof, and that a collateral component going live is not a “token launch.” Those are added burdens the proposition does not impose. The proposition requires an official release and public communication; it does not require cryptographic proof that Polymarket’s key deployed the bytecode or sale/distribution mechanics. Here, Polymarket officially released pUSD in the product sense: it decided to introduce a branded ERC‑20 as platform collateral, documented it, published its address on the “single source of truth,” and announced the production go‑live date. That is official authorship/adoption and a launch communicated to the public. The defense offers no contrary official statement, no alternative issuer attribution, and no timing evidence that undercuts April 28, 2026. Under a preponderance standard, contemporaneous Polymarket‑authored pages and the contracts registry are more than sufficient to cross 50%.

Why this meets the standard
- The only reasonable reading of Polymarket’s own materials is that pUSD was introduced, branded, documented, address‑linked, and made available for platform use on April 28, 2026. That is a token launch by Polymarket, publicly communicated on Polymarket channels.
- The defense’s ambiguity theories (component vs. launch; deployer attribution) describe ways to make the record even stronger, not reasons to find it weaker than 50%. The record already ties the token to Polymarket’s official registry and communications and places the go‑live well before the deadline.

Conclusion
By a preponderance of the evidence, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—and publicly communicated that launch through its own channels. The Council should find the proposition true.

Council,

Standard and burden
The proposition is conjunctive. Plaintiff must prove, by a preponderance of the evidence, that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (1) launched (2) an official (3) fungible token and (4) publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels—where “official fungible token” is defined as a fungible token “officially released by Polymarket” and publicly communicated through its official channels. If any element is not shown to exceed 50%, the claim fails.

What the record does establish (PX‑1, PX‑2)
- Polymarket documentation describes pUSD as a standard, transferable ERC‑20 used as platform collateral and branded “Polymarket USD.”
- A “Contracts” page, styled as the “single source of truth,” lists a pUSD proxy address under Collateral Contracts.
- A changelog and V2 migration page state that a “new collateral token (pUSD)” shipped with CLOB V2 and that production was live April 28, 2026. An explorer page labels the same address (secondary corroboration).

Where the proof falls short of the proposition
1) Launch versus component rollout: The cited pages frame pUSD as part of an infrastructure upgrade (“a new collateral token … shipped with CLOB V2”), not as a stand‑alone token product launch. There is no Polymarket announcement that it “launched a Polymarket token,” and no distribution, supply, listing, or issuance details. On this record, “pUSD became the live collateral component” is at least as plausible as “Polymarket launched a token product.”

2) “Officially released by Polymarket” is unproven: The proposition’s own language requires official release by Polymarket. The record shows adoption and documentation, not authorship or issuance. There is no chain‑level attribution tying deployer, owner, mint/admin authority, or governance of the ERC‑20 to Polymarket. A contracts registry showing what the platform uses proves use; it does not prove that Polymarket released/issued the token. DX‑1 flags these attribution gaps.

3) Communication about what: We do not dispute there was official communication—about the V2 upgrade and the existence/role of pUSD as collateral. What is missing is communication that Polymarket launched and officially released a token, as the proposition requires.

4) Timing does not cure authorship: Even if April 28, 2026 is accepted (and the UTC→Pacific conversion is uncontested), timing only matters if a qualifying launch by Polymarket occurred. The authorship/launch gap remains.

Addressing plaintiff’s strongest point
Plaintiff argues that “shipped a new collateral token” plus production go‑live equals a token launch. That argument reads “officially released by Polymarket” out of the proposition. “Release” connotes issuer‑level action or origination attributable to Polymarket, not merely documenting and enabling a collateral instrument within a backend upgrade. Without issuer attribution or a statement that Polymarket released a token, the evidence is in equipoise between a collateral component activation and a token launch. Under a preponderance standard, equipoise is insufficient.

Exclusions
Whether pUSD is not an NFT, not points, etc., does not resolve the missing elements of launch and official release.

Conclusion
The record demonstrates a Polymarket‑documented, branded ERC‑20 used as collateral and activated with CLOB V2. It does not demonstrate that Polymarket “launched” and “officially released” a token in the sense the proposition requires. Because the key facts are at least equally consistent with a collateral component rollout as with a token launch by Polymarket, plaintiff has not crossed 50%. The Council should find the proposition not proven.

openings.text:
Council, this case asks a narrow, verifiable question under a preponderance standard: Is it more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through its own channels, with NFTs, promotional items, point systems, and partner/community tokens excluded?

Plaintiff’s theory
- Polymarket did launch an official fungible token before the cutoff and said so through its own channels. The decisive proof will pair (1) time‑stamped official communications with (2) on‑chain evidence attributable to Polymarket and (3) indications of public availability consistent with a launch.

What “official fungible token” and “launch” mean
- Fungible token: a transferable cryptoasset on a public blockchain (e.g., ERC‑20/SPL‑like), not an NFT and not non‑transferable points.
- Official: created, issued, or adopted by Polymarket (or its operating entity) and publicly communicated on Polymarket‑controlled channels (website, blog, docs, product UI, verified social, or press releases under its control). Third‑party announcements alone are insufficient.
- Launch: public release—e.g., announcement with a contract address or ticker; enabling transferability, distribution, listing, redemption, or similar availability. Mere internal testing or silent contract deployment is not enough; clear official communication plus observable availability is.

Key factual questions
1) Did Polymarket’s official channels, before the cutoff, communicate that a Polymarket fungible token was launched, naming its ticker or contract?
2) Is there an on‑chain fungible token whose deployer/owner or mint authority is credibly attributable to Polymarket (corporate wallet, multisig, verified contract linked by Polymarket)?
3) Was the token publicly available (listing, distribution, redemption, transfers) before the cutoff?
4) Do materials indicate the asset was instead an NFT, off‑chain/non‑transferable points, a purely promotional voucher, or a partner/community token rather than a Polymarket token?
5) Timing: Do best‑evidence timestamps (official posts, archived pages, block times) place both announcement and availability before the cutoff in PST?

Proof that will satisfy the burden
- Primary: A Polymarket‑controlled announcement before the cutoff explicitly presenting a fungible token as a Polymarket token and identifying its contract or ticker, paired with chain‑explorer data showing the token existed and was usable or distributed before the cutoff.
- Corroboration: Archived snapshots; consistent messaging across official channels; press coverage linking to Polymarket’s announcement; code or repos under Polymarket control referencing the address; attestations tying the deployer to Polymarket.
- Authentication: Evidence tying communicating accounts and deployer wallets to Polymarket (domain control, verified handles, on‑site linking, cryptographic signatures).

Proof that would defeat the claim
- Only points, NFTs, or promotional credits existed before the cutoff; any fungible token appeared only after; or the token was launched by a third party without official Polymarket adoption. Also defeating would be clear official statements before the cutoff that no token had been launched.

Why this resolves the real dispute
- The dispute turns on whether “official” and “launch” are met by Polymarket’s own communications and control over a fungible token, not third‑party hype. The evidence above answers attribution, asset type, and timing against the exclusions.

Next steps to build the record
- Collect and authenticate: (a) Polymarket website/blog/docs/app UI notices; (b) posts from verified official social accounts; (c) archived snapshots prior to the cutoff; (d) chain‑explorer records (contract creation, minting, ownership, distributions) and deployer provenance; (e) official press releases or emails; (f) corroborating reputable press citing Polymarket’s communications. Convert all timestamps to PST and correlate announcement with availability. Obtain cryptographic proofs, if needed, that a deployer or multisig is controlled by Polymarket.

Under a preponderance standard, a coherent set of official communications plus matching on‑chain facts will suffice; the absence of such official communication, or the presence only of excluded categories, will not.

Council, the proposition is conjunctive under a preponderance standard: before 00:00 PST on Jan 1, 2027, Polymarket (1) launched (2) an official (3) fungible token and (4) publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels. NFTs, promos, point systems, and partner/community tokens are excluded. If any element is unproven, the proposition fails.

Defense theory
The more likely scenario is that no such Polymarket‑official fungible token launch—paired with explicit, pre‑cutoff communication by Polymarket—occurred. Items likely to be cited (off‑chain points, per‑market instruments, third‑party or partner tokens, or rumor‑driven posts) either fall within exclusions or fail attribution, launch, or timing.

Disputed elements
- “Official”: whether authorship, control, or adoption is attributable to Polymarket (or its operating entity) rather than unaffiliated deployers or partners.
- “Launch”: whether there was a public release with availability (distribution, transferability, listing) rather than a silent/test deployment.
- “Publicly communicated through official channels”: whether Polymarket’s own site, blog, docs, product UI, or verified social accounts conveyed the launch (third‑party reporting is insufficient).
- Timing: whether both communication and availability pre‑date the cutoff once timestamps are normalized to PST.

Factual questions that will decide the case
1) Did Polymarket itself state, on a Polymarket‑controlled channel before the cutoff, that a Polymarket fungible token had launched, identifying its ticker/contract or role?
2) Is there on‑chain evidence of a fungible token whose deployer/owner or mint authority is credibly attributable to Polymarket (corporate wallet, multisig, domain‑verified contract) before the cutoff?
3) Was the token publicly available pre‑cutoff (transfers enabled, distribution, redemption, or listing), not just inert or test code?
4) Do proffered items fall within exclusions—NFTs, promos, non‑transferable/off‑chain point systems—or are they tokens launched by partners/communities rather than Polymarket?
5) Are there pre‑cutoff official statements by Polymarket indicating no token launch or clarifying that points/promos are not fungible tokens?

Proof that would satisfy plaintiff’s burden
- Primary: A pre‑cutoff Polymarket‑controlled announcement explicitly presenting a Polymarket fungible token as launched, plus chain‑explorer data confirming existence, Polymarket control, and public availability pre‑cutoff. 
- Corroboration: Archived snapshots, consistent cross‑channel messaging, and press that links back to Polymarket’s announcement. 
- Authentication: Ties between deployer/mint authority and Polymarket (domain‑verified addresses, cross‑links from official properties, or cryptographic attestations).

Proof that defeats the proposition
- No pre‑cutoff Polymarket communication announcing a fungible token launch; or communications referencing only excluded categories (NFTs, promos, points). 
- On‑chain tokens bearing “Polymarket” but deployed by unaffiliated third parties/partners without Polymarket’s official adoption and announcement. 
- Pre‑cutoff deployments without Polymarket communication, or with no public availability until after the cutoff. 
- Official Polymarket statements pre‑cutoff that no token had launched or that any referenced asset is not a fungible token.

Record‑building steps
- Collect Polymarket‑controlled materials (website/blog/docs, app UI notices, verified social, official press); preserve timestamps and archives; convert to PST. 
- Obtain chain‑explorer records (creation time, ownership, minting, transfers, verifications); map controlling wallets; look for links from official Polymarket properties. 
- Build a timeline aligning communications with on‑chain facts; note gaps between announcement and availability. 
- Identify and exclude points, NFTs, promos, or partner/community tokens, documenting why each exclusion applies. 
- Search for pre‑cutoff Polymarket statements clarifying absence of a token or non‑fungibility of points.

Why plaintiff is unlikely to prevail
Because the claim requires official authorship/adoption, an actual launch, and explicit pre‑cutoff communication by Polymarket, ambiguity on attribution, asset category, or timing is likely. Under a preponderance standard, unresolved ambiguity on any element defeats the proposition.

case.proposition: Before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token: a fungible token officially released by Polymarket and publicly communicated through official Polymarket channels, excluding NFT launches, promotional offerings, tokenized point systems, and token launches in collaboration with other projects or communities.

rebuttals.text:
Council,

The defense’s case turns on importing requirements that are not in the proposition. The proposition requires: before Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched an (ii) official (iii) fungible token and (iv) publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket channels, while excluding NFTs, promos, points, and partner/community tokens. It does not require (a) an ICO/sale, (b) chain‑level proof that Polymarket’s wallet deployed the contract, or (c) magic words like “token launch.”

1) “Launch” is satisfied by Polymarket’s own go‑live communication and availability
Polymarket’s changelog and V2 migration guide state that a “new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” shipped with CLOB V2 and that production went live on April 28, 2026. Those pages are Polymarket’s official channels and describe a production rollout that made pUSD the live collateral for all trading. Making a new fungible ERC‑20 available for public use on the platform and announcing that fact is a product launch of a token under the ordinary meaning of “launch.” The defense’s demand for issuance/distribution mechanics is extra‑textual. (PX‑1, PX‑2)

2) “Officially released by Polymarket” is met by Polymarket’s attribution and adoption
The Contracts page—explicitly the “single source of truth”—lists pUSD under Collateral Contracts with its Polygon address. Polymarket branded it “Polymarket USD,” documented it, linked to its address, and put it into production as required collateral. That is an official release by Polymarket in the product sense the proposition uses. The proposition does not require cryptographic proof that Polymarket’s key deployed the bytecode; it requires that Polymarket officially release the token and publicly communicate that release. We have both. (PX‑2)

3) Timing is sufficiently proven under a preponderance standard
Polymarket’s dated changelog/migration entries establish an April 28, 2026 production go‑live (~11:00 UTC = 04:00 PDT on April 28, 2026), well before the Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. The defense points to theoretical archival best‑evidence improvements but offers no contrary timeline. Under preponderance, contemporaneous dates on Polymarket‑controlled pages—corroborated by an explorer page for the same address—are enough to cross 50%. (PX‑1, PX‑2; TR‑1 in record)

4) Exclusions do not apply
The record describes pUSD as a standard, transferable ERC‑20 used as trading collateral; it is not an NFT, not a non‑transferable point, not a promo voucher, and not a partner/community token. (PX‑1)

Bottom line
Even accepting the defense’s characterizations of the documents, their own framing concedes a Polymarket‑documented, branded ERC‑20 (“Polymarket USD”) that was shipped and made live on April 28, 2026, with Polymarket publicly communicating that rollout and listing the contract address on its “single source of truth.” That is exactly an official fungible token launched and publicly communicated by Polymarket before the cutoff. The proposition is met by a preponderance of the evidence.

Requested ruling: Find the proposition true.

surrebuttals.text:
Council,

Plaintiff’s rebuttal says we imported extra elements. We did not. The proposition itself narrows the question to whether Polymarket “launched an official fungible token,” further defined as “a fungible token officially released by Polymarket and publicly communicated through official Polymarket channels.” The words “officially released by Polymarket” come from the proposition, not the defense. On this record, authorship/release by Polymarket is unproven.

1) Launch vs. component rollout
Plaintiff equates the April 28, 2026 CLOB V2 go‑live with a token launch. But their own exhibits frame pUSD as a “new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” shipped within an infrastructure upgrade, not as a stand‑alone token product launch. (PX‑1, PX‑2) Nothing in the cited pages says “we launched a Polymarket token.” No distribution, minting, listing, or supply details appear. That leaves “launch” ambiguous between a backend component becoming active and a token product being launched. Under preponderance, that ambiguity matters.

2) “Officially released by Polymarket” remains the missing link
Plaintiff treats documentation and use as proof of release. The Contracts page describes itself as the “single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform” and lists a pUSD proxy address. (PX‑2) That is strong evidence of adoption/integration; it is not evidence that Polymarket authored/deployed the ERC‑20 or holds mint/admin authority. The record contains no chain‑level attribution of deployer, owner, or governance—and DX‑1 flags precisely these gaps (no immutable timestamps, no deployer attribution). On this paper, it is at least equally likely that Polymarket documented and integrated a collateral wrapper as part of V2, rather than “releasing” a Polymarket token in the sense the proposition uses.

3) Communication is conceded, but about what?
Yes, official pages communicate the V2 upgrade and the existence/role of pUSD. (PX‑1, PX‑2) What they do not communicate is that Polymarket launched a token it officially released. The absence of such language and of issuer‑level facts keeps the key element unresolved.

4) Timing does not cure authorship
Even granting April 28, 2026 (TR timing conversion accepted), the failure is not time but authorship/launch.

Bottom line
Plaintiff proved a Polymarket‑documented, branded ERC‑20 used as collateral. They did not prove that Polymarket “officially released” the token or that what occurred was a “token launch” rather than an infrastructure component going live. With the evidence equally consistent with either reading, plaintiff has not crossed 50%. The proposition is not proven.

technical_reports.summary: Polymarket’s April 28, 2026 planned go-live of CLOB V2 at ~11:00 UTC converts to 04:00 Pacific Daylight Time (UTC−7) on April 28, 2026. The proposition’s deadline, January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, is UTC−8. Therefore, the cited launch precedes the deadline by over eight months.
technical_reports.title: Time Normalization (UTC→Pacific) for cited events
